items.question: Where does Generative AI fit in the AI / ML / DL hierarchy?
items.answer: Generative AI is a generative branch of machine learning—systems that sample new data from learned models. It is not a strict fourth layer above deep learning; most modern Gen AI is implemented with deep neural networks, often pretrained via self-supervision. This topic is the canonical taxonomy placement; discriminative vs generative mechanics and model families are in what-is-genai and generative-model-types.

items.question: How is a generative model different from a classifier at inference time?
items.answer: A classifier returns a label or score from a fixed output space. A generative model iteratively samples structured output—tokens, pixels, latents—conditioned on context or noise. The training objective also differs (modeling P(x) or P(x|context) vs P(y|x)), but the deployment distinction is open-ended synthesis versus constrained prediction.

items.question: Is self-supervised learning the same as Generative AI?
items.answer: No. Self-supervised learning is a training paradigm using structure in unlabeled data (next-token prediction, masking, contrastive views). Generative AI describes systems whose primary product is newly generated content. Many Gen AI models are self-supervised during pretraining, but self-supervised encoders like BERT are often deployed discriminatively.

items.question: Why are LLMs discussed separately from this taxonomy topic?
items.answer: This classical-ML framing page establishes vocabulary and placement only. LLMs add scale, tokenization, instruction tuning, context windows, and emergent behaviors—canonical content in llms-fundamentals. Linking avoids duplicating the Gen AI Fundamentals stage on a placement page.
